title: The Wedding Garment
subtitle: The King enters to see the guests, and finds one without the wedding garment

stillness: Be still, and let the King draw near.
He enters to see the guests who recline at the feast.
Let your soul rest in the faith of Christ, and grow quiet.
gospel: The kingdom of heaven is likened to a king who made a marriage for his son.
And he sent his servants to call those that were invited to the wedding.
Behold, I have prepared my dinner; my oxen and fatlings are killed, and all things are ready: come to the marriage.
But they neglected it, and went their ways, one to his farm, another to his merchandise.
And the rest laid hands on his servants, and having treated them with contumely, put them to death.
Then he said to his servants: the marriage indeed is ready, but they that were invited were not worthy.
Go therefore into the highways, and whomsoever you shall find, call to the marriage.
And his servants gathered together all that they found, both bad and good; and the marriage was filled with guests.
And the king went in to see the guests, and he saw there a man not clothed with a wedding garment.
behold: This King is God the Father, who is called King because He reigns in heaven through glory, in the world through grace, in hell through justice.
And He is called man, for the likeness that is between God and man.
He made a threefold marriage for His Son: the first in the womb of the Virgin, in the joining of the divine nature and the human;
the bridal chamber of that wedding, says Gregory, was the virginal womb.
The second He made in the world, in the joining of Christ and the Church.
The third He makes in the faithful friend, in the joining of grace and the believing soul.
The servants He first sent before are the Prophets, who called the invited ones, the Jews, through the Scriptures of the Law.
Then He sent other servants following after: John the Baptist and His Apostles, saying, Tell them that are invited, Behold, I have made ready my dinner.
My oxen, the fathers of the Old Testament, who laboured under the yoke of the Law;
and my fatlings, the fathers of the New Testament, fattened with the richness of charity, winged with the feathers of the virtues, lifted up in mind to the things above, are slain;
for the Saints are killed for the salvation of the people.
But they neglected it, because the Jews despised the preaching of Christ and the Apostles; for few, by comparison, among the Jews believed.
They neglected it, because they feared not the Lord: for he that feareth God neglecteth nothing.

And the King, when He had heard, was wroth, and sending forth His armies, the Romans under Vespasian and Titus, who came by divine ordinance against Jerusalem in the forty-second year after the Passion of Christ, destroyed those murderers and burnt their city.
consider: O most wretched world, and wretched they who follow it!
For worldly works have always shut men out from life.
One went to his farm, another to his merchandise, held back by the ambition of the age, occupied by the greed of money.
And what is far heavier: some, when called, not only refuse the grace, but even persecute it.
John the Baptist they imprisoned and killed; Stephen and James and other disciples likewise.
Now the King has gone in to judgment, for the whole Trinity shall judge, and render to each according to his merits.
He goes in twofold: generally, in the judgment to come; and specially, in the death of every man.
To recline at the marriage of Christ is to partake of the sacraments of the Church, to hold the faith of Christ, and to enjoy the benefits and graces of God.
And He saw there a man, that is, the whole company of the wicked, joined together in one in their malice,
not clothed with the wedding garment, that is, having the faith of Christ, but not the works.
For, according to Jerome, the wedding garment is the commandments of the Lord, and the good works that are done under the Law and the Gospel.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, King of heavenly glory,

who through Thy messengers, that is, through the sacred Scripture, through infirmities, through the peril of living, through the turning of fortune, and through Thy free will, hast called us to Thy marriage:
give us the wedding garment of charity,
that we may never fall silent from the confession of sin and from the praise of God,
that we may never be separated from the resurrection of spiritual joy, that is, from peace and from security;
and give us the garment of piety and of good working,
lest, with hands and feet bound, we be cast into the outer darkness.

latinJewel.latin: O miserrimus mundus, et miseri qui eum sequuntur! Semper enim mundialia opera homines excluserunt a vita.
latinJewel.english: O most wretched world, and wretched they who follow it! For worldly works have always shut men out from life.
